one_line_takeaway: Poisoning a downstream linear head over a frozen contrastive-pretrained encoder is easy only if the poison can look like garbage; enforcing benign, in-distribution poison is hard, and the paper's staged feature-targeted attack narrows but does not close that gap — with transfer learning consistently more vulnerable than fine-tuning and unlearnable examples largely neutralized by a frozen encoder.
key_finding: With a fixed feature extractor, the difficulty of an attack hinges entirely on whether poison must look benign. Unconstrained GC input-space attacks are devastating (fine-tuning -29.54%, transfer -39.35% at only 3% budget) but produce visually garbage, out-of-distribution images (100x-290x larger magnitude than clean) that are trivially sanitized; adding a fidelity/clipping constraint collapses their effect (to -2.50% / -4.40%). The proposed feature-matching FT attack meaningfully beats constrained input-space attacks (e.g. transfer -21.53% vs GC-constrained -4.40% at epsilon_d=0.03, beta=0.25), demonstrating the staged relaxation helps optimization. Two consistent conclusions: (a) transfer learning is markedly more vulnerable than fine-tuning across all attacks, attributed to the pre-train/downstream distribution shift; and (b) exactly inverting a small feature-space perturbation back into a constrained, benign input is genuinely hard — no attack reproduces the target-parameter degradation with visually clean poison. Separately, the Unlearnable Examples (EMN) attack, which is catastrophic in the standard random-init end-to-end regime (-74.84% / -75.53%), becomes weak against a frozen well-pretrained encoder (only -7.55% / -7.88%), suggesting a frozen contrastive encoder is a natural defense against UE.
core_contribution: The paper extends indiscriminate data poisoning attacks (which aim to degrade overall test accuracy, as opposed to targeted or backdoor attacks) from the well-studied end-to-end supervised setting to the setting where a FIXED, trusted, contrastive-learning pre-trained feature extractor f is combined with a trainable linear head h (linear evaluation). The authors' own contribution is twofold: (1) they adapt three existing input-space attacks (TGDA, Gradient Canceling/GC, and Unlearnable Examples/EMN) to only poison the linear head while f is frozen, and (2) they PROPOSE a new class of attacks called Feature Targeted (FT) attacks that decompose the hard constrained optimization into three stages: acquiring a bad target linear-head parameter via GradPC, computing poisoned features via a GC feature-space attack (treating f(mu) as the dataset), then inverting those poisoned features back to the input space via either 'decoder inversion' (a U-Net decoder with a frozen encoder) or 'feature matching' (directly optimizing pixels to match the target feature under an input-similarity penalty, inspired by Shafahi et al.'s feature-collision method). Note: GC, TGDA, GradPC, EMN, and feature-collision are all PRIOR work (Lu et al. 2022/2023a, Sun et al. 2020, Huang et al. 2021, Shafahi et al. 2018); the paper's novel algorithmic contribution is the staged FT attack (decoder inversion + feature matching) plus the empirical characterization of this new threat setting.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section 3.1. Attacker knowledge: access to the training data, the target model including its pre-trained (frozen) feature-extractor weights, and the training procedure (white-box). Capability: can inject a poisoned set into the downstream training data (actively or passively via web scraping), constrained by poisoning fraction epsilon_d = |nu|/|mu|; the Unlearnable Examples (EMN) variant assumes the far stronger epsilon_d = infinity, i.e. the entire training set can be perturbed with small per-sample noise. Objective: indiscriminate — construct clean-label poisoned samples so that after retraining the linear head on the mixed clean+poison set, downstream test accuracy drops. The feature extractor itself is assumed trustworthy/certified and is NOT poisoned (this distinguishes the work from prior SSL-pretraining poisoning like Carlini & Terzis 2021 and He et al. 2022). Attacks generate clean-label poison (only inputs are modified; labels are inherited from clean base images).
limitations: Author-acknowledged: this is a baseline-setting study, not a solved attack — inverting feature-space poison to benign input space remains an open difficulty (called counterintuitive and deserving more study). White-box assumption (attacker has full model/data/procedure access) is strong. Feature matching on transfer learning silently produces a substantial fraction of anomalous, high-magnitude outliers (12.1% at beta=0.25, 18.2% at beta=0.1); a trivial anomaly-removal sanitizer (feature matching-r) guts the transfer-learning attack (from -21.53% to -7.21% at beta=0.25), so the strongest reported numbers depend on the defender NOT sanitizing. Smaller beta trades effectiveness for visibly noisier, human-detectable images. Scope is limited to image classification, CIFAR-10 downstream, two encoders (ResNet-18/50), and only a couple of SSL methods (SimCLR, MoCo v3); no defense beyond simple sanitization/anomaly removal is evaluated, and no possible-defense against EMN is empirically built (left as future work).

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (50k train / 10k test; downstream classification for both scenarios; also SSL pre-training set for the fine-tuning scenario)
ImageNet-1K (unlabeled contrastive pre-training set for the transfer-learning scenario's ResNet-50 encoder)
quantitative_results: Clean linear-eval accuracy: 89.71% (CIFAR-10 fine-tuning, ResNet-18/SimCLR); 93.03% (ImageNet->CIFAR-10 transfer, ResNet-50/MoCo v3)
GC input-space, NO constraints, epsilon_d=0.03: -29.54% (fine-tuning) / -39.35% (transfer) accuracy drop
GC feature-space (intermediate, not a deployable attack), epsilon_d=0.03: -36.61% (fine-tuning) / -43.02% (transfer), near the GradPC target of ~-36.7% / -43.5%
Anomaly-removal defense on transfer feature matching: beta=0.25 drops from -21.53% to -7.21%; beta=0.1 from -33.45% to -6.65% (12.1% and 18.2% of poison points were anomalous)
Unlearnable Examples (EMN, epsilon_d=infinity): original random-init -74.84% (fine) / -75.53% (transfer); end-to-end from pretrained init -26.43% / -29.04%; frozen-encoder input-space -7.55% / -7.88%

Poison magnitude: GC input-space poison is ~100x (fine-tuning) and ~290x (transfer) larger than clean at epsilon_d=0.03, shrinking to ~5x / ~14x at epsilon_d=1; feature matching magnitude ~1x/3x/5.5x normal for beta=0.25/0.1/0.05
baselines_compared: TGDA input-space attack (Lu et al. 2022), with and without constraints
GC (Gradient Canceling) input-space attack (Lu et al. 2023a), with and without constraints
GradPC parameter-corruption target (Sun et al. 2020) — upper-bound reference for reachable degradation
GC feature-space attack — intermediate building block / reference ceiling
Feature matching at beta=0.25/0.1/0.05 (proposed FT variant) and feature matching-r after anomaly removal
Unlearnable Examples / EMN (Huang et al. 2021) in original, end-to-end, and frozen input-space regimes
